Event: Ben sends Ethan and Goodwin to infiltrate the survivors
Explanation: Gives Ben intel and leverage but leads to deaths and terror among survivors.

Event: Ben masquerades as Henry Gale and claims he crashed on the island
Explanation: Deception preserves his cover and control while endangering and destabilizing the survivors.

Event: Ben pretends to be a low-ranking Other after his cover is blown
Explanation: Minimizes his perceived threat and keeps him alive while misleading those holding him.

Event: Ben lies that the real Henry Gale died in the balloon crash
Explanation: Short-term preservation of his fake identity at the cost of trust and clarity for others.

Event: Ben lies again, claiming he didn't personally kill Henry
Explanation: Slight protection from personal blame while further muddying truth for everyone else.

Event: Ben claims the Others' leader is “a brilliant man” to deflect suspicion
Explanation: Tries to distance himself from responsibility while confusing opponents about leadership.

Event: Ben sends Ethan, their chief surgeon, on a dangerous mission despite needing surgery soon
Explanation: Risks losing his only “brilliant” surgeon just after discovering his own cancer, harming both him and his people.

Event: Ben draws an accurate map to the balloon to support his lie
Explanation: Helps sustain his story and survival but sends them on a dangerous wild-goose chase into the jungle.

Event: Ben constantly lies so much that no one believes him when it matters
Explanation: Habitual lying later undermines his own survival and plans and prevents others from avoiding danger.

Event: Ben convinces Sun to trust him when he’s actually telling the truth
Explanation: Accurate information helps both his cooperation strategy and Sun’s safety.

Event: Ben attempts to kill Locke in Season 3 by a single gut shot and leaves him
Explanation: Short-term benefit in removing rival, but sloppy attempt fails and later worsens his own position.

Event: Ben similarly shoots Desmond once in the stomach without confirming the kill
Explanation: Again tries to eliminate a threat but incompetently, gaining little and provoking later resistance.

Event: Ben manipulates survivors in the Hatch, playing Jack and Locke against each other
Explanation: Strengthens his leverage and freedom while increasing division and danger for them.

Event: Ben lies as his first instinct when confronted with problems
Explanation: Short-term escapes accumulate long-term distrust that repeatedly backfires on him.

Event: Ben miscalculates by assuming the flight manifest didn’t survive, leading to Ethan’s exposure
Explanation: Overconfidence gets Ethan killed and undermines Ben’s infiltration strategy.

Event: Ben gambles on reverse psychology with Keamy by denying love for Alex
Explanation: Move catastrophically backfires, getting Alex killed and emotionally destroying Ben.

Event: Ben shows Juliet Goodwin’s dead body to assert ownership of her
Explanation: Intimidates Juliet into compliance at the cost of deep trauma and future resentment.

Event: Ben tries to kill John Locke out of envy when Locke appears chosen
Explanation: Eliminates a rival “chosen one” but increases his own guilt and future problems.

Event: Ben ultimately succeeds in murdering Locke out of petty jealousy
Explanation: Removes a major rival yet crosses a moral event horizon that later torments and weakens him.

Event: Ben helps the Hostiles exterminate the Dharma Initiative during the Purge
Explanation: Secures his place with the Others and power while annihilating his former group.

Event: Ben executes his drunk, abusive father with toxic gas during the Purge
Explanation: Frees himself from abuse and advances with the Others, while still committing patricide.

Event: Ben orders or condones abductions of Claire’s baby and Walt
Explanation: Strengthens his group’s leverage and goals while traumatizing parents and children.

Event: Ben becomes an Enemy Mine / Token Evil Teammate and cooperates with survivors
Explanation: Alliance improves his survival and helps others against larger threats.

Event: Ben tells Jack the freighter people are dangerous and shouldn’t be trusted
Explanation: Accurate warning slightly benefits his agenda but greatly protects survivors.

Event: Ben betrays the Dharma Initiative to join the Others under Widmore
Explanation: Gains status and belonging with the Others by selling out his original group.

Event: Ben betrays Widmore and ousts him to take leadership of the Others
Explanation: Increases his own power at Widmore’s expense and destabilizes leadership dynamics.

Event: Ben eventually betrays Locke, Jacob, and the Man in Black at various points
Explanation: Chronic backstabbing yields short bursts of power that ultimately leave him isolated and used.

Event: Ben keeps a shotgun hidden in his piano bench
Explanation: Increases his personal safety with little cost to others unless misused.

Event: Ben brainwashes Karl in Room 23 to stop him impregnating Alex
Explanation: Tries to control Alex’s life for his own parental/security neurosis, harming Karl’s autonomy.

Event: Ben refuses to kill Penny in front of her young son Charlie and spares her
Explanation: Abandons revenge (self-cost in his vendetta) to avoid traumatizing a child and killing an innocent.

Event: Ben allows his daughter to die by choosing power games over surrendering
Explanation: Attachment to control directly results in Alex’s death and his ongoing guilt.

Event: Ben kills Keamy in a rage after Keamy murders Alex, despite knowing about the dead man’s switch
Explanation: Gets revenge but triggers many additional deaths and undermines the rescue effort.

Event: Ben summons the Smoke Monster to slaughter the mercenaries
Explanation: Protects himself and companions through extreme, indiscriminate violence.

Event: Ben kidnaps infant Alex rather than kill her and raises her as his daughter
Explanation: Spares her life but robs her of parents and uses her within his agenda.

Event: Ben threatens to kill Karl’s relationship with Alex by controlling them
Explanation: Feeds his possessive control at the cost of their autonomy and happiness.

Event: Ben lies to Jack and Locke about drawing the map so they’ll fear an ambush
Explanation: Gains amusement and psychological leverage while increasing their anxiety for no gain to them.

Event: Ben withholds information about Naomi slipping away to mess with Jack
Explanation: Enjoys trolling and momentary advantage while obstructing Jack’s situational awareness.

Event: Ben lies casually about his mother teaching him to read
Explanation: Minor identity bluff slightly amuses/empowers him while misleading others needlessly.

Event: Ben claims to Locke that he’s a Pisces despite actually being a Sagittarius
Explanation: Purely playful deception that mildly gratifies him while giving Locke false trivia.

Event: Ben attacks Ana Lucia after she killed two Others, calling them “good people”
Explanation: Retaliates for his people’s deaths and asserts a moral high ground while fueling conflict.

Event: Ben beats the highly trained Keamy in a straight fight and stabs him to death
Explanation: Eliminates a lethal enemy but knowingly endangers everyone on the freighter.

Event: Ben refuses to kill innocent people when possible, even while at war
Explanation: Foregoing easy brutality preserves some moral line and reduces collateral harm without clear downside.

Event: Ben pushes Hurley out of the way of a falling tree, getting trapped himself
Explanation: He is injured and trapped, but Hurley’s life is saved and others later free Ben.

Event: Ben gives Islanders and Alex’s safety priority over external threats for years
Explanation: Devotes his life to protecting his group, often via violent, harmful means to outsiders.

Event: Ben decides to remain in the Flash-Sideways to work things out and look after Alex
Explanation: Accepts extra time in limbo to continue caring for Alex and improve himself, benefiting both.

Event: Ben kills Jacob after being told he was never special
Explanation: Satisfies his wounded ego and removes a superior but severs his protection and empowers worse forces.

Event: Ben chooses not to become Man in Black’s Dragon permanently and instead plots against him
Explanation: Feigned allegiance preserves his survival while aiding the true heroes’ plan.

Event: Ben re-joins the survivors once MiB leaves with Jack and Desmond
Explanation: Aligning with them increases his safety and directly supports efforts to stop MiB.

Event: Ben tells Kate where Man in Black’s escape boat is
Explanation: Helps them escape and reach the final confrontation, while aligning him with the winning side.

Event: Ben pushes Hurley out of danger then serves as Hurley’s loyal Number Two
Explanation: Finds meaningful, stable purpose while strengthening Hurley’s leadership for the Island’s good.

Event: Ben respectfully closes Horace’s eyes after the Purge
Explanation: Gains nothing tangible but shows remorse and humanity, slightly soothing his own conscience and honoring the dead.

Event: Ben tutors Alex as a caring teacher in the flash-sideways
Explanation: Helps Alex academically and emotionally while giving himself a healthier, redemptive role.

Event: Ben shares a candy bar with Hurley, starting their odd friendship
Explanation: Fosters trust and camaraderie that later benefit both of them and the Island.

Event: Ben protects Sun from Flocke and is honest with her about Jin
Explanation: Supports his redemption, gains trust, and materially helps Sun’s safety and understanding.

Event: Ben decides not to “move on” yet in the afterlife because he feels he has more to work out
Explanation: Takes extra time to grow and atone, aiding his own development and indirectly those he cares about.
